description: To establish and maintain one's location and direction while moving through a physical or informational place. Landmarks and markers may help; so may memory, touch, sound, or assistance. 'kulo' (guide) names leading by an agent; 'runaki' keeps the traveller as the person finding the way.
articulatory_notes: Make a voiced tap or short trill with the tongue tip and draw the tongue back into high-back rounded 'u'. For dental 'n', rest the tip against the backs of the upper teeth, carry the voice through the nose, and open into stressed central 'a'. Close at the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The word moves from a low roll through a steady stressed center to one bright, crisp endpoint. Its changing points of contact can recall a route found cue by cue rather than seen all at once.
usage_notes: The verb is intransitive. An instrumental phrase may name markers, memory, touch, sound, a device, or another person's information; ordinary spatial phrases can add a route or destination. Noun use gives wayfinding as an activity. Assistance does not move agency from the traveller to the source of the cue.

examples.translation: I find my way using the markers.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A public path works differently when its turns can be found without requiring one familiar guide beside every traveller.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The verb preserves the traveller's direction over the act even when another person or device supplies information.

semantic_domains.cognition: using cues to establish location and direction
semantic_domains.spatial: finding a route through a physical or informational place
